subtopics.title: Comprehensive Chemistry Revision
common_misconceptions: SS3 chemistry is entirely new (it's mainly revision and practical)
Organic chemistry is the hardest topic
subtopics.teaching_note: WAEC Chemistry format: Paper 1 (Obj, 50 questions), Paper 2 (Essay, 5 questions), Paper 3 (Practical, 3 questions). Practical skills: volumetric analysis (titration), qualitative analysis (salt analysis), general practical. Titration tips: read burette at eye level, remove funnel before titrating, use white tile, repeat for concordant values (within 0.10 cm³). Salt analysis: systematic procedure (appearance, dilute HCl, NaOH, NH₃, flame test). Common cations: Pb²⁺, Cu²⁺, Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺, Al³⁺, Ca²⁺, Zn²⁺, NH₄⁺. Common anions: CO₃²⁻, SO₄²⁻, Cl⁻, NO₃⁻.

subtopics.title: Systematic Salt Analysis
common_misconceptions: Adding excess reagent doesn't matter (it does - some precipitates dissolve in excess)
Flame test is unreliable (it's a quick preliminary test)
subtopics.teaching_note: Preliminary: Note color of salt (Cu²⁺ = blue/green, Fe²⁺ = pale green, Fe³⁺ = brown, Ni²⁺ = green). Flame test: Na⁺ (yellow), K⁺ (lilac), Ca²⁺ (brick red), Cu²⁺ (blue-green). Action of dilute HCl: CO₃²⁻ (effervescence, CO₂ turns limewater milky), SO₃²⁻ (SO₂ smell). Action of NaOH: Cu²⁺ (blue ppt, insoluble in excess), Fe²⁺ (green ppt → brown), Fe³⁺ (red-brown ppt), Al³⁺ (white ppt, soluble in excess), Zn²⁺ (white ppt, soluble in excess), Pb²⁺ (white ppt, soluble in excess), Ca²⁺ (white ppt, insoluble in excess). NH₃ similar to NaOH but Al³⁺ and Pb²⁺ don't dissolve in excess NH₃.
